part_b_answer: This is polymorphism. Both Dog and Cat inherit from Animal and each provides its own implementation of is_senior(). When adoption_candidates calls a.is_senior(), Python looks at the actual runtime type of the object and dispatches to the correct version automatically. That's why the function works on a mixed list without any isinstance checks — it just calls the shared method and lets each class handle the logic.

The principle is polymorphism. Each subclass overrides is_senior() with its own threshold (8 for dogs, 10 for cats), but because both classes share the Animal interface, the adoption_candidates function can call is_senior() on any element without knowing what type it is. Python resolves which method to run at runtime based on the object's actual class. This also means if you added a Rabbit class in the future with a different threshold, the function would still work without any changes.

Polymorphism makes this work. Dog and Cat each override is_senior() differently, but since both are Animal subclasses, the function treats them the same way through the shared interface. Python dispatches to the right is_senior() at runtime depending on what kind of object it actually is. The function doesn't care about the different thresholds — each class manages its own logic internally.

This is polymorphism. Even though Dog uses 8 and Cat uses 10 as the senior threshold, both classes share the is_senior() method name. Python knows at runtime which version to call based on the object's type, so the function works correctly on both without any type-checking.

The OOP principle is polymorphism. The function calls is_senior() on each animal and Python runs the correct version depending on whether it is a Dog or Cat. This is why it works even though the thresholds are different — each class handles the details and the function just uses the shared interface.
